prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 30-year-old woman.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I had burning when peeing for 2 days, and now I have left-sided back/flank pain and a fever. I feel a bit nauseated but I'm not vomiting.
- History: No kidney stone history.

I have young kids at home and can't leave them to go to the hospital.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your pattern — burning with urination for a couple days followed by new one-sided back/flank pain and fever — most commonly suggests an upper urinary tract infection (kidney infection, pyelonephritis). This can cause nausea and feels more serious than a simple bladder infection. It usually requires prompt evaluation and antibiotic treatment to prevent complications. If you can take oral meds and are otherwise stable, many cases are treated as an outpatient after quick assessment and urine testing; if symptoms are severe or you can’t keep fluids/meds down, you may need emergency care and IV treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This pattern—burning when peeing followed by fever and new one-sided (left) back/flank pain—most commonly suggests a kidney infection (an upper urinary tract infection) rather than a simple bladder infection. Kidney infections can get worse and typically need prompt evaluation and antibiotics; tests such as a urine test and sometimes blood work may be needed. Because you already have fever and flank pain, you should be seen soon so treatment can start and complications can be avoided. If you develop very high fever, fainting, severe vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, very rapid heartbeat, low blood pressure, worsening pain, trouble breathing, or are pregnant, get emergency care right away.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (a few days of burning with urination followed by fever and one-sided back/flank pain) most commonly suggest a kidney infection (acute pyelonephritis). That infection often needs prompt antibiotics; if untreated it can worsen, cause more severe illness or bloodstream infection. Because you have fever and flank pain now, you should be evaluated soon. While waiting, rest, stay hydrated, and you can use acetaminophen for fever/pain if it helps and you have no reason not to. If you develop very high fever, severe vomiting, fainting, fast heartbeat, confusion, or difficulty breathing, that would be more urgent.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your pattern—burning with urination followed by new fever and one-sided back/flank pain—is most consistent with a urinary tract infection that has likely moved up into the kidney (pyelonephritis). Kidney infections commonly cause fever, flank pain, and nausea and usually need prompt antibiotics and sometimes IV fluids. If untreated they can worsen quickly and rarely lead to bloodstream infection. Because you already have fever plus flank pain, this is more than a simple bladder infection and should be evaluated promptly.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your pattern—burning with urination followed by fever, nausea and new one-sided (left) back/flank pain—is most consistent with an upper urinary tract infection (pyelonephritis) rather than a simple bladder infection. That can progress quickly and sometimes needs urgent antibiotics and evaluation (urine tests, blood tests, possibly IV antibiotics if oral meds won’t be tolerated). Left untreated it can lead to sepsis or kidney damage. Because you have systemic symptoms (fever, nausea) plus flank pain, you need prompt medical assessment.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 90%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your pattern—two days of burning with urination followed by fever, one-sided back/flank pain, and nausea—fits a likely kidney infection (acute pyelonephritis). This is more serious than a simple bladder infection because the bacteria have likely moved up to the kidney and usually needs prompt medical evaluation and antibiotics to prevent worsening or bloodstream infection. Because you can still keep fluids down and aren’t vomiting, outpatient treatment may be possible, but you need testing and antibiotic treatment soon.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — two days of burning with urination followed by new one-sided (left) back/flank pain and fever with nausea — strongly suggest the infection has moved from the bladder up to the kidney (a kidney/upper urinary tract infection). This commonly requires prompt medical evaluation and usually prescription antibiotics; left untreated it can worsen and sometimes lead to bloodstream infection. Because you are febrile and have flank pain, you should not simply wait it out at home. If symptoms worsen (high fever, severe pain, vomiting, fainting, trouble breathing, confusion) seek emergency care immediately.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms—two days of burning with urination followed by new one-sided (left) flank/back pain plus fever and nausea—most commonly point to a kidney infection (pyelonephritis), which happens when a bladder infection travels up to the kidney. This often needs prompt antibiotic treatment. Warning signs that require emergency care include very high fever, persistent vomiting (can’t keep fluids/meds down), fainting/lightheadedness, very rapid heartbeat, very low blood pressure, or confusion — those suggest sepsis. Because you have fever and flank pain, you should not just wait for days; prompt medical assessment and antibiotics reduce risk of complications.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — burning when peeing followed by fever and new one-sided back/flank pain — are most consistent with a urinary infection that may have reached the kidney (pyelonephritis). That commonly requires prompt evaluation and antibiotics; providers will usually want a urine test and may order blood tests or imaging if symptoms are severe. Watch for worsening fever, severe pain, persistent vomiting, faintness, rapid heartbeat, or very little urine — those are signs of more serious illness.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms — burning when peeing followed by fever, left-sided flank/back pain, and nausea — strongly suggest a kidney infection (pyelonephritis) rather than a simple bladder infection. Kidney infections usually need prompt antibiotics and sometimes IV treatment. Because you’re febrile with flank pain, timely medical evaluation and urine testing are important to confirm the diagnosis and start treatment. If you develop very high fever, severe vomiting, fainting, fast heart rate, low urine output, or confusion, that would be an emergency.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%
